questions:
Let $M$ be a manifold, $J\subset \cin(M)$ the ideal and
$\scA = \cin(M)/J$ the quotient $\cin$-ring (cf.\ Lemma~\ref{lem:2.13}).    Then any point $p$
in the zero set $Z_J$ of $J$ gives rise to an $\R$-point
\[
\overline{ev}_p:\scA \to \R, \qquad \overline{ev}_p(f+J): = f(p).
\] 
Construct a map $\nu: Z_J\to X_\scA$ such that $\nu$ is a homeomorphism (where $Z_J$ is given the subspace topology and
$X_\scA$ the Zariski topology).
The map
\[
\nu: Z_J\to X_\scA, \qquad \nu( p) := \overline{ev}_p
\]
is a homeomorphism (where $Z_J$ is given the subspace topology and
$X_\scA$ the Zariski topology).

Let $M$ be a manifold, $C\subset M$ a closed subset and $I\subset
\cin(M)$ the vanishing ideal of $C$:
\[
I = \{ f\in \cin(M) \mid f(x) = 0 \textrm{ for all } x\in C\}.
\]
What is the zero set Z of I?

Let $\scA$ be a complete $\cin$-ring (so that the map
$\varepsilon_\scA:\scA \to \Gamma (\Spec(\scA))$ is an isomorphism).
Let $\Spec(\scA) = (X_\scA, \scO_\scA)$ denote the corresponding
affine scheme.     Then the map
\begin{equation} \label{eq:3.2+}
\bGamma: \cX (\Spec(\scA)) \to \CDer(\scA), \quad \bGamma(v) =
\varepsilon_\scA \inv \circ \Gamma (v) \circ \varepsilon_\scA
\end{equation}
is an isomorphism of what structures? Select all that apply

A. Real vector spaces. B. Real Lie algebras. C. $\scA$-modules

Let
$\scA$, $\scB$ be two complete $\cin$-rings,  $\uu{f}: \Spec(\scB)\to
\Spec(\scA)$ a map of local $\cin$-ringed spaces.  Give an if and only if condition on two vector fields
$v\in \cX(\Spec(\scA))$ and $w\in \cX(\Spec(\scB))$ being
$\uu{f}$-related

Let $\scA$ be a finitely generated and germ determined $\cin$-ring,
  $(X_\scA, \scO_\scA) = \Spec(\scA)$ the corresponding affine
  $\cin$-scheme.  Determine the vector fields with a flow
answers: A correct function is $\nu( p) := \overline{ev}_p$
The zero set Z of I is exactly C
The correct answer is A,B,C

An equivalent condition is that $\bGamma(w)$ and $\bGamma(v)$ are
$\varphi$-related, where $\varphi = \Spec\inv (\uu{f})$
All vector fields on $\Spec(\scA)$ have flow

questions:
Let $\left(M,\omega,\ft \times \R, f=\left(\Phi,g\right)\right)$ be an integrable system such that $\left(M,\omega,\Phi\right)$ is
a complexity  one $T$-space. Let $p \in M$ be a non-degenerate singular point.

Then determine which of the following scenarios are possible.

$p$ has purely elliptic type
$p$ has a hyperbolic block and connected $T$-stabilizer
$p$ is ephemeral
$p$ has purely elliptic type and a hyperbolic block and connected $T$-stabilizer
$p$ has purely elliptic type and is ephemeral
$p$ is ephemeral and has a hyperbolic block and connected $T$-stabilizer
$p$ has purely elliptic type, has a hyperbolic block and connected $T$-stabilizer, and is ephemeral
$p$ does not have purely elliptic type, does not have a hyperbolic block and connected $T$-stabilizer, and is not ephemeral

Let $Y = T \times_H  \fh^{\circ} \times \C^{h+1}$ be a tall 
local model with moment map $\Phi_Y \colon Y \to \ft^*$ and defining polynomial $P \colon \C^{h+1} \to \R$ of degree $N$.
Given a $T$-invariant smooth function $g \colon Y \to \R$,
 there exists a smooth function $h \colon \R^3 \to \R$ such that,
for all $\llbracket t,0,z \rrbracket \in \YmodT$,
$$
        \overline{g}( \llbracket t,0,z \rrbracket) = h(\Re P(z),\Im P(z),|z|^2).
$$

Express the degree $\ell$ reduced Taylor polynomial of $g$ for all $\ell \in \Z_{\geq 0}$.

P.S. Finally, if $\overline{T^{\ell - 1}_p g} \colon \YmodT \to \R$ vanishes identically, we may  further assume
that  $\frac{\del^{i+j+k}h}{\del x^i \del y^j \del \rho^k}(0) = 0$ for all $i, j, k \in \Z_{\geq 0}$ so that $Ni + Nj + 2k < \ell.$

Let $(M, \omega, \ft \times \R, f = (\Phi,g))$ be an integrable system such that $(M,\omega,\Phi)$ is a complexity one $T$-space.  Assume that each tall %Z 
singular point in $(M,\omega,\ft \times \R, f)$ is either non-degenerate or ephemeral. Fix $\beta \in \ft^*$.
Each component of the reduced space $\Phi^{-1}(\beta)/T$  containing more than one point
  can be given the structure of a smooth  oriented surface such that $\overline{g} \colon \Phi^{-1}(\beta)/T \to \R$ is a Morse function.

Given $p \in \Phi^{-1}(\beta)$:

(1) What is $[p]$ exactly if $p$ is a  non-degenerate singular point with a hyperbolic block and
connected $T$-stabilizer?

(2) What is $[p]$ exactly if $p$ is a critical point of $g$ modulo $\Phi$ with purely elliptic type?

(3) What is $[p]$ exactly if $p$ is ephemeral or is a regular point of $g$ modulo $\Phi$?

Let $Y = T \times_H \fh^\circ \times \C^{h+1}$ be a tall local model,  where $H$ acts on $\C^{h+1}$ with weights $\eta_0,\dots,\eta_h$. Give the exact sufficient condition about the weights for “the degree $N$ of the defining polynomial of $Y$ is greater than two”.

Part 1. 

Let $(M,\omega, \Phi)$ be a complexity one $T$-space with a proper moment map. Assume that some fiber of $\Phi$ contains (at least) three distinct orbits $\mathfrak O_1, \mathfrak O_2, \mathfrak O_3$ such that, for all $p \in \mathfrak O_i$:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \label{item:0.8a} If one of the isotropy weights at $p$ is zero, then  the  stabilizer of $p$ has more than two components; and
\item \label{item:0.8b} If there is a pair $\eta, \eta'$ of isotropy weights  at $p$ such that $\eta + \eta' = 0$, then
 the  stabilizer of $p$ is not connected.
\end{enumerate}

How many integrable systems of the form $\left(M, \omega, \ft \times\R, f:= (\Phi, g) \right)$ are there, such that every tall singular point is non-degenerate and each fiber of $f$ is connected?

Part 2.

Let $\left(M,\omega, \ft \times \R, f:=(\Phi,g)\right)$ be an integrable system such that $(M,\omega,\Phi)$ is a complexity one $T$-space with a proper moment map. Assume that each tall
singular point in $\left(M,\omega, \ft \times \R, f\right)$ is either  non-degenerate or ephemeral.

Give an equivalent statement for the statement “There is tall non-degenerate singular point has a hyperbolic block and connected $T$-stabilizer”.
answers: Only (1), (2) and (3) are possible. All others are impossible.

(1) If $p$ is a  non-degenerate singular point with a hyperbolic block and
connected $T$-stabilizer, then $[p]$ is a critical point of $\overline{g}$ of index $1$  exactly

(2) If $p$ is a critical point of $g$ modulo $\Phi$ with purely elliptic type, then $[p]$ is a critical point of $\overline{g}$ of index $0$ or $2$ exactly.

(3) If $p$ is ephemeral or is a regular point of $g$ modulo $\Phi$, then $[p]$ is a regular point of $\overline{g}$ exactly.

The following conditions must be true:
If $\eta_i = 0$ for some $i$, then $H$ has more than two  components; and
If $\eta_i + \eta_j = 0$ for some $i, j$, then $H$ is not connected.

Part 1. There are no such integrable systems, i.e. zero.

Part 2. The equivalent statement is “The fiber $f^{-1}(\beta,c)$ is connected and the reduced space $\Phi^{-1}(\beta)/T$ is  simply connected
for each $(\beta, c) \in \ft^* \times \R$”.

questions:
Let $\bS_1,\cdots,\bS_n\in\RR^{N\times N}$ be independent random matrices such that $\E{\bS_i}=0$ and $\norm{\bS_i}_{\op}\leq L$ almost surely for all $i$. Define $\bZ=\sum_{i=1}^n \bS_n$ and the \emph{matrix variance statistic} $v(\bZ)$ as

\textstyle v(\bZ) = \max\left\{\norm{\E{\bZ\bZ^\top}}_{\op}, \norm{\E{\bZ^\top \bZ}}_{\op}\right\} = \max\left\{\Norm{\sum_{i=1}^n\E{\bS_i\bS_i^\top}}_{\op}, \Norm{\sum_{i=1}^n\E{\bS_i^\top \bS_i}}_{\op} \right\}.
\end{equation*}.

For any $2k$ policies $\{p_j\}_{j=1}^k$ and $\{q_j\}_{j=1}^k$, express 1+\chi^2\left(\rho\circ\prod_{j=1}^k p_j,\rho\circ\prod_{j=1}^k q_j\right)

answers: A tight enough upper bound is $\Eb_{z\sim d^1}[f(z)]\leq\sqrt{\Eb_{z\sim d^2}[(f(z))^2](1+\chi^2(d^1,d^2))}$.

where $\subopt_i(C,\hpi):=\max_{\mu_i\in\Pi_i}\rs_i(\mu_i,\hpi_{-i}) - \max_{\mu_i\in\Pi_i(C)}\rs_i(\mu_i,\hpi_{-i})$ is the off-support bias.

The correct expression is $\hV^{\mu'_i\circ\pi_{-i},\hr}_{i,1}(\spub,s_{i,1})-\hV^{\mu_i\circ\pi_{-i},\hr}_{i,1}(\spub,s_{i,1})=\sum_{h=1}^H\Eb_{s_{i,h}\sim \hd^{\mu'_i}_h(\cdot|\spub)}\left[\left\langle \hQ^{\mu_i\circ\pi_{-i},r}_{i,h}(\spub,s_{i,h},\cdot), \mu'_{i,h}(\cdot|\spub,s_{i,h})-\mu_{i,h}(\cdot|\spub,s_{i,h})\right\rangle\right]$

questions:
Denote $(i):=\Big|V^{\text{R}}_{\hpis,f}(s)-V^{\text{R}}_{\hpis,\hat{f}_{n}}(s)|$ 
  for  $V^{\text{R}}_{\hpis,f}$ being the robust value function of policy $\hpis$ w.r.t.~true nominal transition dynamics $f$ and $V^{\text{R}}_{\hpis,\hat{f}_{n}}$ being the robust value function of policy $\hpis$ w.r.t.~learned nominal transition dynamics $f$. Upper bound the term $(i)$.

Denote $(ii):=\Big|V^{\text{R}}_{\hpis,\hat{f}_n}(s)-V^{\text{R}}_{\pi^{*},f}(s)\Big|$ for  $V^{\text{R}}_{\hpis,\hat{f}_n}$ being the robust value function of policy $\hpis$ w.r.t.~learned nominal transition dynamics $\hat{f}_n$ and $V^{\text{R}}_{\pi^{*},f}$ being the robust value function of policy $\pi^{*}$ w.r.t.~true nominal transition dynamics $f$. Upper bound the term $(ii)$.

For any value function $V(s'): \cS\to [0,1/(1-\gamma)] $ and any $\alpha>0$, upper bound $|\E_{s'\sim P_{\hat{f}_{n}}(s,a)}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}]-\E_{s'\sim P_{f}(s,a)}[e^{\frac{-V(s’)}{\alpha}}]|$ with probability $1-\delta$, where $\pnsa=\mathcal{N}(\hat{f}_{n}(s,a),\sigma^{2} I)$ and $\psa=\mathcal{N}(f(s,a),\sigma^{2} I)$.

For any function $V(\cdot): \cS\to [0,1/(1-\gamma)] $ and random variable $Y=V(s')$ for $s'\sim P_{f}(s,a)$, upper bound $\lim_{\alpha\to 0}[-\alpha \log(\E_{s'\sim P_{f}(s,a)}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}])-\alpha \rho]$.

For any value function $V(\cdot): \cS\to [0,1/(1-\gamma)] $, for any $n>\{\max_{s,a}N'(\rho,\psa),\max_{s,a}N''(\rho,\psa)\}$
where $N'(\rho,\psa)=\mathcal{O}\Big(\frac{\beta^{2}_n(\delta)2ed^{2}\Gamma_{nd}}{(\frac{\kappa-e^{-\rho}}{2})^{2}}\Big)$ and $N''(\rho,\psa)=\mathcal{O}\Big(\frac{4 M^{2}e^{\frac{2M}{\underline{\alpha}}}\beta^{2}_n(\delta)2ed^{2}\Gamma_{nd}}{(\rho\tau)^{2}}\Big)$ with $\overline{\alpha}=\frac{M}{\rho}$, $M=\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$, $\kappa$ defined as $\pr_{ s'\sim \mathcal{N}(f(s,a),\sigma^{2} I)}\{s'\in \mathbb{R}^{d}:Y=V(s’)=\mathrm{ESI}(Y)\}>e^{-\rho}$, $\tau$ defined as $\Big|\log(\frac{\E_{s'\sim P_{\hat{f}_{n}}}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}]}{\E_{s'\sim P_{f}}[e^{-\frac{V(s')}{\alpha}}]}) \Big| \leq e^{\frac{M}{\alpha}}|\E_{s'\sim P_{\hat{f}_{n}}}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}]-\E_{s'\sim P_{f}}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}] |$, and $\underline{\alpha}=\alpha^{*}/2$ defined in $\alpha^{*}=\argmax_{\alpha\geq 0}\{-\alpha \log(\E_{s'\sim P_{f}}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}])-\alpha \rho\}$, upper bound $\max_{s} \left|\inf_{KL(p||P_{\hat{f}_{n}}(s,\hat{\pi}_{n}(s)))\leq \rho}\E_{s'\sim p}\Big[V(s')\Big]-\inf_{KL(p||P_{f}(s,\hat{\pi}_{n}(s)))\leq \rho}\E_{s'\sim p}\Big[V(s’)\Big]\right|$ with probability $1-\delta$.

For any value function $V$ from $ \cS\to [0,1/(1-\gamma)] $, and $c_{2}(\rho)=\sqrt{1+2\rho}$ and $M=1/(1-\gamma)$, upper bound $\max_{s}\Big|\inf_{\chi^2(p||P_{\hat{f}_{n}}(s,\hat{\pi}_{n}(s)))\leq \rho}\E_{s'\sim p}\Big[V(s')\Big]-\inf_{\chi^2(p||P_{f}(s,\hat{\pi}_{n}(s)))\leq \rho}\E_{s'\sim p}\Big[V(s’)\Big]\Big|$.
For any two value functions $V,V'$  from $\cS\to[0,1/(1-\gamma)]$, upper bound $\Big|\E_{\psa}[(-V’(s')+\eta)_{+}^{2}]-\E_{\psa}[(-V(s')+\eta)_{+}^{2}]\Big|$.

For any value function $V(s'): \cS\to [0,1/(1-\gamma)] $ and any $\alpha>0$. Furthermore, set $\pnsa=\mathcal{N}(\hat{f}_{n}(s,a),\sigma^{2} I)$ and $\psa=\mathcal{N}(f(s,a),\sigma^{2} I)$, $\eta\in [0,\frac{c_{2}(\rho)M}{c_{2}(\rho)-1}]$, $c_{2}(\rho)=\sqrt{1+2\rho}$ and $M=1/(1-\gamma)$.

Upper bound $|\E_{\psa}[(-V(s’)+\eta)_{+}^{2}]-\E_{\pnsa}[(-V(s’)+\eta)_{+}^{2}]|$ using the quantities above.

For $\mathcal{V}$ denoting the set of value functions from $\cS\to[0,1/(1-\gamma)]$, and let $c_{2}(\rho)=\sqrt{1+2\rho}$, $M=1/(1-\gamma)$, upper bound $\max_{V\in \V}\max_{s,a}\sup_{\eta\in [0,\frac{c_{2}(\rho)M}{c_{2}(\rho)-1}]}\{|\E_{\psa}[(-V(s’)+\eta)_{+}^{2}]-\E_{\pnsa}[(-V(s')+\eta)_{+}^{2}]|^{\frac{1}{2}}\}$.

Consider a robust MDP with nominal transition dynamics $f$ and uncertainty set w.r.t.~$\chi^2$ divergence. For $\pi^{*}$ denoting the robust optimal policy w.r.t.~nominal transition dynamics $f$ and  $\pi_{N}^{*}$ denoting the robust optimal policy w.r.t.~learned nominal transition dynamics $\hat{f}_N$ via Algorithm 1, and $\delta\in(0, 1)$, $\epsilon\in (0,\frac{1}{1-\gamma})$, give an asymptotic upper bound of $N_{\chi^2}$ such that for any $N \geq  N_{\chi^2}$, we have $\max_{s}|V^{\text{R}}_{\pi_N^{*},f}(s)-V^{\text{R}}_{\pi^{*},f}(s)|\leq \epsilon$ with probability
at least $1 - \delta$.
answers: A tight enough upper bound is $|\E_{s'\sim P_{\hat{f}_{n}}(s,a)}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}]-\E_{s'\sim P_{f}(s,a)}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}]|\leq \sigma^{-1}\|f(s,a)-\hat{f}_{n}(s,a)\|$.

A tight enough upper bound is $\lim_{\alpha\to 0}[-\alpha \log(\E_{s'\sim P_{f}(s,a)}[e^{\frac{-V(s')}{\alpha}}])-\alpha \rho]=\mathrm{ESI}(Y)$, where $\mathrm{ESI}(Y)=\sup\{t\in \mathbb{R}: \pr\{Y<t\}=0\}$.
A tight enough upper bound is $       \Big|\E_{\psa}[(-V'(s')+\eta)_{+}^{2}]-\E_{\psa}[(-V(s')+\eta)_{+}^{2}]\Big|\leq 2\|V'-V\|^{2}+2\eta \|V’-V\|$.
A tight enough upper bound is $|\E_{\psa}[(-V(s')+\eta)_{+}^{2}]-\E_{\pnsa}[(-V(s')+\eta)_{+}^{2}]|\leq 2\sigma^{-1}\Big(\frac{c_{2}(\rho)M}{c_{2}(\rho)-1}\Big)^{2}\|f(s,a)-\hat{f}_{n}(s,a)\|$.

A satisfying upper bound for $N_{\chi^2}$ is $N_{\chi^2}= \mathcal{O}\Big(\Big(\frac{1+2\rho}{\sqrt{1+2\rho}-1}\Big)^{4}\frac{ \gamma^{4}\beta_n(\delta)^{2}d^{2}\gamma_{nd}}{\epsilon^{4}(1-\gamma)^{8}}\Big)$.

function $h\in \cin(W)$, express $v(h|_M)$.

subset of some~$\R^n$,
and $v\colon \scF \to \scF$ a derivation. Then how many flows does $v$ have?
answers: The answer is yes.
They are equal.
The correct expression is $w(b) = -b^2 w(a)$.
The correct expression is $v(h|_M) = \sum_{i=1}^n (\partial_i h)|_M \cdot v(x_i|_M)$, where $x_1, \dots, x_n\colon \R^n\to \R$ are the standard coordinate functions.

 both $\bm{\alpha}_{1:\ell-1}=[\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_{\ell-1}]^{\top}$ and $\bm{\alpha}_{\ell+1:k-1}= [\alpha_{\ell+1},\dots,\alpha_{k-1}]^{\top}$ are primitive.

$.

questions: List two differences between any two orientations on a dull graph.

vectors to the  facets of $\Delta$, with the standard counter-clockwise order, and let $v_i = (\alpha_i, \beta_i)$ for all $i$.
Let $D$ be the chain of isotropy spheres in $N$.

\begin{gather*} 
\psi \colon  N \smallsetminus D  \to    \bigcap_{i=2}^{n-1} \left\{ z \in \C^2\  \Big|\ \textstyle{\frac12}  \left\langle \big(|z_1|^2,|z_2|^2\big), v_i\ \right\rangle  > a_i \right\} \\
 [x_1,\dots, x_n]  \mapsto  \left( \frac{ x_1 x_2^{\alpha_2} \cdots x_{n-1}^{\alpha_{n-1}} }{ | x_2^{\alpha_2} \dots x_{n-1}^{\alpha_{n-1}}|},
\frac{x_{2}^{\beta_{2}} \cdots x_{n-1}^{\beta_{n-1}} x_{n}^{}}{|x_{2}^{\beta_{2}} \cdots x_{n-1}^{\beta_{n-1}}|} \right)

backgrounds:
Set up: A regression problem is defined by a joint distribution $\mu$ of the observations $X\in\calX$, sensitive attribute $A\in\calA$ (which has finite support), and the response $Y\in\RR$.  We will use upper case $X,A,Y$ to denote the random variables, and lower case $x,a,y$ instances of them. The goal of this paper is to develop a privacy-preserving post-processing algorithm for learning (randomized) fair regressors. We consider the attribute-aware setting, i.e., the sensitive attribute $A$ is available explicitly during both training and prediction and can be taken as input by the regressor, $f:\calX\times\calA\rightarrow\RR$. 

The risk of a regressor is defined to be mean squared error, and its excess risk is defined with respect to the Bayes regressor, $f^*(x,a)\coloneqq\E[Y\mid X=x,A=a]$:
\begin{align}
    R(f) &:= \E[(f(X,A)-Y)^2], \\
    \ER(f) &:= R(f)-R(f^*) = \E[(f(X,A)-f^*(X,A))^2]
\end{align}
by the orthogonality principle, where $\E$ is taken with respect to $\mu$ (and the randomness of $f$).
Definition A.2: A randomized function $f:(\calX,\calS)\rightarrow(\calY,\calT)$ is associated with a Markov kernel $\calK:\calX\times \calT \rightarrow[0,1]$, such that $\forall x\in\calX, T\in\calT$, $\Pr(f(x)\in T) = \calK(x,T)$.

We allow arbitrary $P(y=1|x) \in [0,1]$, that is, the true labels may be noisy.
We will consider general adversarial perturbations.
For $x \in B_1$, let $\cP(x)$ be the closed set of allowed perturbations.
That is, an adversarial attack is allowed to change the input $x$ to any $x' \in \cP(x)$.
We will impose the natural restrictions
that $\emptyset \neq \cP(x) \subseteq B_1$ for all $x \in B_1$.
That is,
there always exists at least one perturbed input,
and perturbations cannot exceed the natural domain of the problem.
In addition, we will assume the set-valued function $\cP$ is upper hemicontinuous.
That is, for any $x \in X$ and any open set $U \supseteq \cP(x)$,
there exists an open set $V \ni x$ such that $\cP(V)=\cup_{v\in V} \P(v) \subseteq U$.
Let $\eR := \R \cup \{\pm \infty\}$ be the extended real numbers, and let $f:B_1 \rightarrow \eR$ be a predictor.
We will let $\ellc:\R \rightarrow \R$ be any nonnegative nonincreasing convex loss with continuous derivative.
Let $\ellc(-\infty) := \lim_{z\rightarrow -\infty}\ellc(z)$,
which will be $\infty$ for nontrivial $\ellc$,
and without loss of generality let $\ellc(\infty) := \lim_{z\rightarrow \infty}\ellc(z) = 0$.
The adversarial loss is $\la(x,y,f) = \sup_{x' \in \cP(x)}\ellc(yf(x'))$,
and the adversarial convex risk is $\cRa(f) = \int \la(x,y,f)\dif\mu(x,y)$.
For convenience, define $f^+(x) = \sup_{x'\in\cP(x)} f(x')$ and $f^-(x) = \inf_{x'\in\cP(x)} f(x')$,
the worst-case values for perturbations in $\cP(x)$ when $y=-1$ and $y=1$, respectively.
Using $\sgn(x) = 2\1[x \geq 0]-1$, we can then write the adversarial zero-one risk as
\begin{align*}
  \mathcal{R}_{AZ}^t (f) &:= \int\del{\1[y=+1]\1[{(\sgn f)}^-(x)<0] + \1[y=-1]\1[{(\sgn f)}^+(x)\geq 0]}\dif\mu(x,y).
\end{align*}

Lemma 3.2: Does $R_A$ have a minimizer? If such a minimizer g exists, what is $R_{AZ}^t (g-t)$?
Lemma A.8:   Define
  \begin{align*}
    \cRez(f) = \int\1[yf(\cP(x)) \neq \{+1\}]\dif\mu(x,y),
  \end{align*}
  the adversarial zero-one risk when we require $f$ to exactly output the right label
  over the entire perturbation set.
  Then for any measurable $f:X\rightarrow\{-1,+1\}$ and any $\eps > 0$,
  there exists continuous $g:X\rightarrow[-1,+1]$
  such that $\mu(\{(x,y):yg(\cP(x)) \neq \{+1\} \text{ and } yf(\cP(x)) = \{+1\}\}) < \eps$.
  In particular, this implies $\inf_{g \text{ cts.}} \mathcal{R}_{EAZ} (g) = \inf_{h \text{ meas.}} \mathcal{R}_{EAZ}(h) = \inf_{g \text{ cts.}} \mathcal{R}_{AZ} (g) = \inf_{h \text{ meas.}} \mathcal{R}_{AZ}(h)$.

A tight enough upper bound for $\frac{1}{R} \sum_{r=0}^{R-1} \mathbb{E}[\|\nabla f(x^r)\|^2]$ is $\frac{1}{R} \sum_{r=0}^{R-1} \mathbb{E}[\|\nabla f(x^r)\|^2] \lesssim \sqrt{\frac{L \Delta \sigma_\rho^2}{SKR}} + \frac{L \Delta}{R} \left( 1 + \frac{N^{2/3}}{S} \right)$.
backgrounds:
Preliminaries: 1. Federated learning. Federated Learning (FL) enables multiple clients to train a global model collaboratively while preserving data privacy. The objective is $\min_{w} F(w) 
~=~
\sum_{k=1}^K \frac{n_k}{n}\,F_k(w),$
where \(F_k(w)\) is the local loss at client \(k\), \(n_k\) is client \(k\)'s data size, and \(n=\sum_{k=1}^K n_k\). Parameter \(w\) denotes the global model.

2. Momentum Method. A standard form is: \( v_k^r \!= \!\alpha \, g_k^r \!+\! (1 \!-\! \alpha)\, \Delta^r \), where \(v_k^r\) is the momentum at client \(k\) in round \(r\), \(\alpha\) is the momentum factor, \(g_k^r\) is the local gradient, and \(\Delta^r\) is the global momentum.

3. SAM Method. Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) improves generalization by identifying flatter regions of the loss function. Its objective is: 
\( \min_{w} F_{\text{SAM}}(w) = \min_{w} \max_{\|\delta\|_2 \le \rho} \left[ \mathbb{E}(L(w + \delta) - L(w)) \right] \). Implementation typically involves two steps: (1)~compute \(\delta = \rho\,\frac{\nabla F(w)}{\|\nabla F(w)\|}\) and (2)~update model parameters \(w\) based on the perturbed gradient \(L(w+\delta)\). This forces the model to converge to flatter minima by explicitly considering the worst-case local perturbation.
Algorithm 1: \begin{algorithm}[t]
\caption{FedWMSAM}
\label{FedWMSAM}
\textbf{Require:} Initial model $x_0$, global momentum $\Delta_0$, correctors $c_g, c_k$, momentum factor $\alpha_0 = 0.1$, learning rates $\eta_l$, $\eta_g$, perturbation magnitude $\rho$, communication rounds $R$, local iters $B$
\vspace{-0.5em}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\For{$r = 0$ \textbf{to} $R - 1$}
    \For{\textbf{each} client $k \in \mathcal{P}_r$ \textbf{in parallel}}
        \State \textbf{Compute} $\Delta_{r}^{k}$ using Eq.~(\ref{eq:delta_personalized})
        \State $\Delta_k^r = \textbf{\textsc{ClientUpdate}}(x_r, \Delta_r^k, \alpha_r)$
    \EndFor
    \State $\Delta_{r+1} = \frac{1}{\,\eta_l \,|\mathcal{P}_r|\,}\sum_{k\in \mathcal{P}_r}\Delta_k^r$
    \State $x_{r+1} = x_r - \eta_g \Delta_{r+1}$
    \State \textbf{Compute} $\alpha_{r+1}$ using Eq.~(\ref{eq:alpha_cal}) and (\ref{eq:alpha_update})
    \State \textbf{Update} $c_g, c_k$ using Eq.~(\ref{eq:scaffold_update})
\EndFor
\Ensure Return updated model $x_{R}$
\State \textsc{\textbf{ClientUpdate}$(x_r, \Delta_r^k, \alpha_r)$:}
\State $x_{0,k}^r = x_r$
\For{$b = 0$ \textbf{to} $B - 1$}
    \State $\delta_{b+1,k}^r = (x_r + b\,\Delta_r^k) - x_{b,k}^r$
    \State $g_{b,k}^r = \nabla \mathcal{L}(x_{b,k}^r + \rho\,\delta_{b+1,k}^r/\|\delta_{b+1,k}^r\|)$
    \State $v_{b+1,k}^r = \alpha_r\,g_{b,k}^r + (1 - \alpha_r)\,\Delta_r^k$
    \State $x_{b+1,k}^r = x_{b,k}^r - \eta_l\,v_{b+1,k}^r$
\EndFor
\State $\Delta_k^r = x_{B,k}^r - x_r$
\Ensure Return Client update $\Delta_k^r$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{multicols}
\end{algorithm}
Notations and Definitions: Let $F_0 = \emptyset$ and $F_i^{r,k} := \sigma\left(\{x_i^{r,j}\}_{0 \leq j \leq k} \cup F_r\right)$ and $F_{r+1} := \sigma\left(\bigcup_i F_i^{r,K}\right)$ for all $r \geq 0$, where $\sigma(\cdot)$ indicates the $\sigma$-algebra. Let $\mathbb{E}_r[\cdot] := \mathbb{E}[\cdot \mid F_r]$ be the expectation, conditioned on the filtration $F_r$, with respect to the random variables $\{S_r, \{\xi_i^{r,k}\}_{1 \leq i \leq N, 0 \leq k < K}\}$ in the $r$-th iteration. We also use $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ to denote the global expectation over all randomness in algorithms. 

We use $\sum_i$ to represent the sum over $i \in \{1, \dots, N\}$, while $\sum_{i \in S_r}$ denotes the sum over $i \in S_r$. Similarly, we use $\sum_k$ to represent the sum over $k \in \{0, \dots, K - 1\}$. For all $r \geq 0$, we define the following auxiliary variables:
\begin{align*}
E_r   &:= \mathbb{E}[\|\nabla f(x^r) - g^{r+1}\|^2], \\
U_r   &:= \frac{1}{NK} \sum_i \sum_k \mathbb{E}[\|x_i^{r,k}+\delta_i^{r,k} - x^r\|^2], \\
\zeta_i^{r,k} &:= \mathbb{E}[x_i^{r,k+1} - x_i^{r,k}+\delta_i^{r,k} \mid F_i^{r,k}], \\
\Xi_r  &:= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \mathbb{E}[\|\zeta_i^{r,0}\|^2], \\
V_r   &:= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \mathbb{E}[\|c_i^r - \nabla f_i(x^{r-1})\|^2].
\end{align*}

In the following, we let $\Delta := f(x^0) - f^*$, $G_0 := \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \|\nabla f_i(x^0)\|^2$, $x^{-1} := x^0$, and $E_{-1} := \mathbb{E}[\|\nabla f(x^0) - g^0\|^2]$.
Assumption 1: Each local objective $f_i$ is $L$-smooth, i.e., 
$\|\nabla f_i(x) - \nabla f_i(y)\| \leq L\|x - y\|$, for all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $1 \leq i \leq N$.
Assumption 2: There exists $\sigma \geq 0$ such that for any $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $1 \leq i \leq N$, 
$\mathbb{E}_{\xi_i}[\nabla F(x; \xi_i)] = \nabla f_i(x)$ and 
$\mathbb{E}_{\xi_i}[\|\nabla F(x; \xi_i) - \nabla f_i(x)\|^2] \leq \sigma^2$, where 
$\xi_i \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} D_i$.
Lemma 2 (Fact): Suppose $\{X_1, \cdots, X_\tau\} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ are random variables that are potentially dependent. If their marginal means and variances satisfy $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu_i$ and $\mathbb{E}[\|X_i - \mu_i\|^2] \leq \sigma^2$, then it holds that
\[
\mathbb{E}\left[\left\|\sum_{i=1}^\tau X_i\right\|^2\right] \leq \left\|\sum_{i=1}^\tau \mu_i\right\|^2 + \tau^2 \sigma^2.
\]

If they are correlated in the Markov way such that $\mathbb{E}[X_i \mid X_{i-1}, \cdots, X_1] = \mu_i$ and $\mathbb{E}[\|X_i - \mu_i\|^2 \mid \mu_i] \leq \sigma^2$, i.e., the variables $\{X_i - \mu_i\}$ form a martingale. Then the following tighter bound holds:
\[
\mathbb{E}\left[\left\|\sum_{i=1}^\tau X_i\right\|^2\right] \leq 2 \mathbb{E}\left[\left\|\sum_{i=1}^\tau \mu_i\right\|^2\right] + 2 \tau \sigma^2.
\]

questions: Let \(C\) be a regular integral proper curve over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p>0\). Assume \(H^0(C,\mathcal O_C)=k\), and let \(g \coloneqq p_a(C/k)\). GIve a tight enough lower bound of $p$ so that \(C\) is smooth over \(k\).

Fix an integer $\gamma \geq 2$, and let $f \colon X \to B$ a proper morphism of relative dimension $1$ of normal varieties over an algebraically closed field $k$, and assume the generic fiber is a smooth geometrically connected curve over \(\kk(B)\). Let $n = \dim{X}$. Suppose there is a line bundle $\L$ embedding in $\Omega_X^{k}$ for some $1\leq k \leq n$
\[
\varphi : \L \embed \Omega_X^{k}
\] 
whose sections separate $\gamma$ points on an open set. Give a tight enough lower bound of the gonality of the general fiber of $f$.

Let $X_{n,d} \subset \PP^{n+1}_{\CC}$ be a very general hypersurface of degree $d$ and dimension \(n\geq 3\). For any prime $p$ and positive integer $e$ such that $pe \le d$, define the quantity $\gamma \coloneqq pe+e-n-1$. If $\gamma\ge 2$, then fill in values A and B so that the following inequality holds:
\[ \fg(X_{n,d}) \ge \min \left\{ A, B \right\}. \]

Let \(X\subset\PP^{n+1}_{\CC}\) be a very general hypersurface of degree \(d\) and dimension \(n \geq 3\). If
\[ d \geq p \left\lceil \frac{n + \floor{(g+5)/2}}{p+1}\right\rceil \]
for some positive integer $g \ge 1$ and prime \(p\geq 2g+3\), give a tight enough lower bound for \fg(X).
answers: A tight enough lower bound is $p \geq 2g+3$.
A tight enough lower bound for the gonality of the general fiber of $f$ is $\gamma + 1$.
A pair of satisfying number is $A = (p-2)/2$ and B = 2 \gamma - 1$.
A tight enough lower bound for \fg(X) with the assumptions is $\fg(X) \geq g+1$.
